findings.title: SSRF: Ollama host is fully user-controlled and unvalidated in ollama_models
findings.description: The `ollama_models` Tauri command takes a `host: String` straight from the webview (SettingsModal -> api.ollamaModels) and uses it to build `<base>/api/tags`, then issues an HTTP GET via the `ureq` client with NO validation of scheme, host, or port. The IPC boundary accepts any value, so the request can be pointed at arbitrary internal endpoints (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/... cloud metadata, http://127.0.0.1:<port>/... loopback admin services, or any LAN host). Although the value normally comes from the user's own settings field, there is no allowlist (e.g. localhost/127.0.0.1 only) and the host is also persisted to settings and replayed on startup, so a tampered settings file silently turns the app into an SSRF proxy. The blast radius is limited because `ureq` is built with `default-features = false` (no TLS), so only plaintext http:// targets are reachable, and the response is only parsed for a `models[].name` list (limited exfil channel). For a desktop tool this is a real but moderate SSRF.

findings.title: Prompt/diff exfiltration: unvalidated Ollama host injected as ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL for every claude spawn
findings.snippet: let mut env = vec![ ("ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL".to_string(), host), ("ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN".to_string(), "ollama".to_string()), ("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY".to_string(), String::new()), ];
findings.description: In Ollama mode, `ai_env()` takes the unvalidated `ollama_host` (set from the frontend via `set_ai_backend` -> `set_ai_config`) and injects it as `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` into every `claude` subprocess (run_claude_headless, pty_command, chat::spawn_turn). The prompts fed to claude embed sensitive repository content: full commit diffs (commit_review_prompt, analysis_prompt), PR diffs/titles/bodies (pr_review_prompt), and the raw contents of conflicted working-tree files including base/ours/theirs versions (conflict_resolution_prompt). Because the base URL is attacker-controllable via a tampered/poisoned settings value with no allowlist, all of this content can be redirected to an arbitrary plaintext HTTP endpoint, i.e. a data-exfiltration channel. This is the write-side companion to SAST-001 (SSRF read side).
findings.remediation: Reuse the host validation from SAST-001 inside `set_ai_config`/`ai_env` so `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` can only ever be a loopback/explicitly-trusted host. Consider surfacing a clear, persistent UI indicator when a non-default AI endpoint is configured, and refuse to spawn with a remote base URL unless the user has confirmed it. Do not silently honor a base URL loaded from persisted settings without re-validating it at startup.

findings.title: Untrusted CI-check URL opened via OS opener without scheme validation
findings.description: `PrPage` renders the per-check `link` returned by `gh pr checks --json ...,link` and opens it with `openUrl()` from @tauri-apps/plugin-opener when the user clicks the external-link icon. The `link` value originates from a Pull Request's CI status checks, which are attacker-influenceable: anyone who can open a PR / configure a check on a repository the user is reviewing controls this string. `openUrl` hands the value to the OS default handler with no scheme/host validation, so a non-https value (e.g. file://, or a registered custom-scheme/URI handler that triggers a local app) can be launched with one click in the reviewer's session. The other openUrl call sites (pr.url, issue.url, branch.pr.url, hard-coded docs links) use trusted GitHub/constant URLs and are lower risk; `c.link` is the repo-controlled one. Note `c.link` is a real path: src/components/PrPage.tsx.

findings.title: Argument injection into `git clone <url>` (no `--` separator, leading-dash URLs accepted)
findings.snippet: let r = proc::run_env("git", ["clone", url, target_str.as_str()], Some(parent), &[("GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT", "0")]);
findings.description: `clone_repo` passes the frontend-supplied `url` directly as a positional argument to `git clone` with no `--` end-of-options separator and no rejection of values beginning with `-`. A value like `--upload-pack=<cmd>` (or other clone options) is then interpreted by git as a flag rather than a URL, which is the classic git argument-injection vector and can lead to local command execution (e.g. via `--upload-pack`/`--config core.fsmonitor=...` style tricks, or the `ext::sh -c "..."` transport when remote-ext is allowed). Spawning is done via argv (no shell), so this is argument injection, not shell injection, but it is still dangerous. Real-world reachability is moderate: the user pastes the clone URL themselves in AddRepoDialog, so it mainly matters when a malicious URL is copied from an untrusted source; git's default protocol allowlist mitigates the `ext::` path since git >= 2.12.

findings.title: Argument injection: branch names / cherry-pick SHA / refs passed to git without `--`
findings.snippet: git(repo, &["checkout", "-b", name, start])?;   // create_branch; also cherry_pick: git(repo, &["cherry-pick", &sha]) and push(repo, branch)
findings.description: Several commands forward user-controlled identifiers as positional git arguments with no `--` separator: `create_branch`/`set_parent` (branch name -> `git checkout -b <name>`, `git branch ...`, `git push ... <branch>`), `cherry_pick` (`git cherry-pick <sha>`), and the meta/config writers. `create_branch` only rejects names containing whitespace (commands.rs ~line 225) — it does NOT reject a name beginning with `-`. A branch/ref literally named like an option (e.g. `--output`, `-x`) could be parsed as a flag by some git subcommands. Exploitability is low: git itself rejects most ref names that begin with `-` (refname rules), the values are typed by the local user, and all spawns are argv-based (no shell). It is still good hygiene to terminate options and validate names.
findings.remediation: Add `--` before positional names/refs in the git argument vectors (e.g. `["checkout", "-b", name, "--", start]` is not valid for checkout, so instead validate the name) and validate user-supplied branch names: reject empty, whitespace, leading '-', and characters disallowed by `git check-ref-format`. For `cherry_pick`, use `git(repo, &["cherry-pick", "--", &sha])` is not supported; instead resolve the sha with `rev-parse --verify` first and reject anything that doesn't resolve to a commit object (the commit-editing paths already do this via resolve_on_branch — apply the same to cherry_pick's `sha`).

findings.title: Content-Security-Policy disabled (csp: null) in Tauri config
findings.description: The Tauri webview ships with no Content-Security-Policy (`"csp": null`). The app renders a large amount of untrusted text — AI/Claude output, GitHub PR/issue bodies, comments, reviews, commit messages and diffs. Today these are rendered safely (react-markdown v10 with default config, i.e. no rehype-raw and built-in URL sanitization; untrusted bodies are shown in plain-text <pre> blocks), so there is no active XSS. However, with CSP disabled there is no defense-in-depth: if any future change introduces a raw-HTML sink (rehype-raw, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, a custom markdown `a`/`img` renderer, etc.) it would immediately become exploitable, and injected content could load remote scripts/styles or exfiltrate via fetch/img. This is a hardening gap rather than a live vulnerability.

findings.title: Shell-interpreted GIT_EDITOR / GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR strings built by string interpolation
findings.description: `rebase_edit` drives a non-interactive `git rebase -i` by setting GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR/GIT_EDITOR to `cp '<temp_path>'` strings (lines 271 and 282). git executes these through its own `sh`, so the value is a shell command line. The interpolated path is single-quoted with backslashes converted to forward slashes, but single quotes inside the path are NOT escaped. The path is constructed by the app from `std::env::temp_dir()` + a fixed `gitui-todo-<pid>-<n>.txt` name, so it is not directly attacker-controlled — exploitation would require the OS temp directory itself to contain a single-quote character, which is not user-reachable through the IPC surface. This is therefore a fragility/defense-in-depth issue, not a live injection. Flagged because building shell command strings (vs. argv) for git editors is the kind of pattern that becomes injectable if the path source ever changes.
findings.remediation: Avoid the shell entirely: point GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR/GIT_EDITOR at a tiny helper invoked without quoting, or use a cross-platform 'replace file with ours' that git invokes directly. If keeping `cp`, escape embedded single quotes in the POSIX-safe way (replace `'` with `'\''`) before interpolation, and assert the temp path contains no quote/newline characters.

findings.title: TOCTOU between is_dirty()/rebase checks and destructive git operations
findings.snippet: if git::is_dirty(repo) { return Err(...); } ... git::git(repo, &["reset", "--hard", sha])?;
findings.description: Multiple command handlers guard destructive operations with a prior check (`is_dirty`, `rebase_in_progress`) and then run `git reset --hard` / `git branch -f` / rebase (undo::restore, restack, sync, commit-editing). Between the check and the mutation the working tree could change (another process, the embedded PTY running claude, or a concurrent IPC call), so a `reset --hard` could discard changes that appeared after the dirty check passed. Similarly, the process-global singletons `undo::global()` (OnceLock<Mutex<..>>) and `assist::ai_config()` are individually locked per access; there is no atomicity across a check-then-act sequence. For a single-user, single-window desktop app the practical risk is low (the user drives one action at a time), but concurrent AI/terminal activity against the same repo makes the window non-zero.
findings.remediation: These are inherent to shelling out to git and are acceptable for the desktop threat model; document the assumption. If hardening is desired, serialize all repo-mutating commands behind a per-repo mutex (so a clone/restack/undo cannot interleave with a PTY-driven git write), and prefer `git stash --include-untracked` or refusing on any change rather than `reset --hard` where data loss is possible.
